---
title: "From Bhutan to Texas: why I chose to pursue a PhD after a decade in the field"
description: "After eight years leading conservation programs in Bhutan, why go back to university? This is my attempt to answer that question honestly."
author: "Indra P. Acharja"
date: "2023-09-01"
categories: [Reflections, Conservation, PhD life]
image: ""
---

In July 2023 I left Bhutan and arrived in San Marcos, Texas, to begin a doctoral program at Texas State University. It was a stranger transition than I had anticipated — from running a team of conservationists and managing an $8 million program portfolio, to being a graduate student again, sitting in classes and reading literature on topics I had been living in the field for a decade.

People ask me why. It is a fair question.

## What the field taught me

I spent eight years working for the Royal Society for Protection of Nature in Bhutan, almost all of it focused on the White-bellied Heron. In that time, I helped design and implement recovery plans, led population surveys, established the world's first ex-situ population of the species, mobilized millions of dollars for conservation, and built partnerships across governments, international NGOs, and local communities.

I also accumulated a growing list of questions I didn't know how to answer.

What is the actual probability that the wild population survives the next 20 years, under different conservation scenarios? What is the demographic rate most limiting population growth — is it adult survival, or breeding success, or juvenile recruitment? What would it take to identify reintroduction sites and assess their suitability? How do we build an AI monitoring system robust enough to actually scale across three countries?

These questions require methods I had encountered but not mastered: integrated population models, Bayesian statistics, bioacoustics analysis, remote sensing. Answering them properly required more than field experience. It required time to think, access to expertise, and the structure to do the work rigorously.

## What the PhD offers

The honest answer is that a PhD is a protected space to ask hard questions carefully.

In conservation practice, there is always another crisis, another funding deadline, another meeting. The work is important but it rarely creates the conditions to stop and think deeply about method and inference. A doctoral program forces that. It gives you time to read, to be wrong, to rebuild, to be wrong again in a more productive direction.

It also gives you collaborators. Clay Green's lab at Texas State works at the intersection of avian ecology and conservation — exactly where my questions live. The combination of his expertise in waterbird biology and the broader resources of the university's biology department has already pushed my thinking in directions I couldn't have reached alone.

## What I hope to bring back

I have never thought of the PhD as a departure from applied conservation. I think of it as a detour that makes the applied work better.

The models I build during this program should inform the management of the wild White-bellied Heron population directly. The monitoring systems we develop should be deployable by RSPN and partner agencies in Bhutan and India. The papers I write should contribute to the literature that guides how conservationists think about small-population management for critically endangered birds.

I will return to that work. I am just taking the time, for a few years, to learn how to do it more precisely.

If you are working on White-bellied Heron conservation, or on related problems in avian ecology or freshwater biodiversity in the Himalayas, I would very much like to hear from you: [indra.acharja@txstate.edu](mailto:indra.acharja@txstate.edu).
